course.content:
Les logs Linux suivent le format **syslog** :
```
Jan 15 10:23:45 hostname processus[PID]: message
```

Fichiers de logs critiques pour un analyste SOC :

| Fichier | Contenu |
|---------|--------|
| `/var/log/auth.log` | Authentifications, sudo, SSH |
| `/var/log/syslog` | Messages système généraux |
| `/var/log/kern.log` | Logs du noyau Linux |
| `/var/log/apache2/access.log` | Requêtes HTTP Apache |
| `/var/log/nginx/error.log` | Erreurs Nginx |
| `/var/log/ufw.log` | Firewall UFW |

```python
import re
from collections import Counter, defaultdict
from datetime import datetime
```

```python
import re
from collections import Counter

def analyser_auth_log(lignes):
    """
    Analyse les tentatives SSH dans auth.log.
    Retourne les statistiques et les attaquants.
    """
    echecs = []      # (timestamp, user, ip)
    succes = []      # (timestamp, user, ip)
    
    # Patterns de log SSH
    p_echec  = re.compile(r"(\w+ \d+ \d+:\d+:\d+).*Failed password for (\S+) from (\d+\.\d+\.\d+\.\d+)")
    p_succes = re.compile(r"(\w+ \d+ \d+:\d+:\d+).*Accepted password for (\S+) from (\d+\.\d+\.\d+\.\d+)")
    p_invalid = re.compile(r"Failed password for invalid user (\S+) from (\d+\.\d+\.\d+\.\d+)")
    
    for ligne in lignes:
        m = p_echec.search(ligne)
        if m:
            echecs.append({"ts": m.group(1), "user": m.group(2), "ip": m.group(3)})
            continue
        m = p_succes.search(ligne)
        if m:
            succes.append({"ts": m.group(1), "user": m.group(2), "ip": m.group(3)})
    
    return echecs, succes

# Test avec logs simulés
logs_simules = [
    "Jan 15 10:00:01 srv sshd[123]: Failed password for root from 185.220.101.3 port 45100 ssh2",
    "Jan 15 10:00:02 srv sshd[124]: Failed password for root from 185.220.101.3 port 45101 ssh2",
    "Jan 15 10:00:05 srv sshd[125]: Accepted password for admin from 192.168.1.5 port 52000 ssh2",
    "Jan 15 10:00:10 srv sshd[126]: Failed password for invalid user oracle from 194.165.16.11 port 51200 ssh2",
]

echecs, succes = analyser_auth_log(logs_simules)
print(f"Échecs SSH : {len(echecs)}")
print(f"Succès SSH : {len(succes)}")
attaquants = Counter(e["ip"] for e in echecs)
for ip, n in attaquants.most_common():
    print(f"  {ip}: {n} tentatives")
```

exercises.title: Extraire les IPs des échecs SSH
exercises.description: Parser des logs auth.log pour extraire les IPs des échecs d'authentification
exercises.instructions:
Soit ces logs :
```python
logs = [
    "Jan 15 10:00:01 srv sshd: Failed password for root from 185.220.101.3 port 45100",
    "Jan 15 10:00:02 srv sshd: Accepted password for admin from 192.168.1.5 port 52000",
    "Jan 15 10:00:03 srv sshd: Failed password for oracle from 194.165.16.11 port 51200",
]
```
Extrayez les IPs des lignes `Failed password` et affichez :
```
Attaquants : ['185.220.101.3', '194.165.16.11']
```
exercises.explanation: Le filtre `"Failed password" in log` pré-filtre avant la regex, ce qui améliore les performances sur de gros fichiers.

exercises.title: Détecter un brute force
exercises.description: Compter les tentatives SSH par IP et alerter si seuil dépassé
exercises.instructions:
Soit :
```python
logs = [
    "Jan 15 10:00:01 srv sshd: Failed password for root from 185.220.101.3",
    "Jan 15 10:00:02 srv sshd: Failed password for admin from 185.220.101.3",
    "Jan 15 10:00:03 srv sshd: Failed password for test from 185.220.101.3",
    "Jan 15 10:00:04 srv sshd: Failed password for root from 10.0.0.1",
]
```
Affichez les IPs avec **3 tentatives ou plus** :
```
Brute force : 185.220.101.3 (3 tentatives)
```
exercises.correction:
import re
from collections import Counter
logs = [
    "Jan 15 10:00:01 srv sshd: Failed password for root from 185.220.101.3",
    "Jan 15 10:00:02 srv sshd: Failed password for admin from 185.220.101.3",
    "Jan 15 10:00:03 srv sshd: Failed password for test from 185.220.101.3",
    "Jan 15 10:00:04 srv sshd: Failed password for root from 10.0.0.1",
]
ips = [re.search(r"from (\d+\.\d+\.\d+\.\d+)", l).group(1) for l in logs if "Failed" in l]
for ip, n in Counter(ips).items():
    if n >= 3:
        print(f"Brute force : {ip} ({n} tentatives)")
exercises.explanation: Counter + seuil est la technique de base pour la détection de brute force. En production, on ajoute une fenêtre temporelle pour éviter les faux positifs sur de longues périodes.

exercises.title: Identifier les utilisateurs visés
exercises.description: Extraire les usernames ciblés dans les tentatives SSH
exercises.instructions:
Depuis ces logs, extrayez les usernames **uniques** ciblés dans les `Failed password` :
```python
logs = [
    "Jan 15 sshd: Failed password for root from 10.0.0.1",
    "Jan 15 sshd: Failed password for admin from 10.0.0.1",
    "Jan 15 sshd: Failed password for root from 185.220.101.3",
    "Jan 15 sshd: Failed password for postgres from 185.220.101.3",
]
```
Affichez le nombre de comptes ciblés uniques :
```
Comptes ciblés : 3
```
exercises.correction:
import re
logs = [
    "Jan 15 sshd: Failed password for root from 10.0.0.1",
    "Jan 15 sshd: Failed password for admin from 10.0.0.1",
    "Jan 15 sshd: Failed password for root from 185.220.101.3",
    "Jan 15 sshd: Failed password for postgres from 185.220.101.3",
]
users = set()
for log in logs:
    m = re.search(r"Failed password for (\S+) from", log)
    if m:
        users.add(m.group(1))
print(f"Comptes ciblés : {len(users)}")
exercises.explanation: Un set élimine automatiquement les doublons. Analyser les comptes ciblés (root, admin, postgres) révèle les services vulnérables exposés sur le serveur.
